perturbation.original_text: EACH PARTY HERETO HEREBY WAIVES TO THE FULLEST EXTENT PERMITTED BY APPLICABLE LAW ANY RIGHT IT MAY HAVE TO A TRIAL BY JURY WITH RESPECT TO ANY LITIGATION DIRECTLY OR INDIRECTLY ARISING OUT OF, UNDER OR IN CONNECTION WITH THIS AGREEMENT OR ANY OF THE TRANSACTIONS CONTEMPLATED HEREBY.
perturbation.changed_text: EACH PARTY HERETO WAIVES ANY RIGHT IT MAY HAVE TO A TRIAL BY JURY WITH RESPECT TO ANY LITIGATION DIRECTLY OR INDIRECTLY ARISING OUT OF, UNDER OR IN CONNECTION WITH THIS AGREEMENT OR ANY OF THE TRANSACTIONS CONTEMPLATED HEREBY.
perturbation.explanation: By removing 'TO THE FULLEST EXTENT PERMITTED BY APPLICABLE LAW', the clause now appears to be an absolute waiver of jury trial rights, without regard to legal limitations that might exist. Some jurisdictions may not enforce jury waivers under certain circumstances, or may require specific language ensuring voluntariness and informed consent. This omission creates a legal risk, as the waiver could be deemed unenforceable under certain state laws.
perturbation.contradicted_law: New York Civil Practice Law & Rules (CPLR) § 2201
perturbation.law_explanation: NY CPLR § 2201 discusses discretionary stays of trial, implying that there are scenarios where a trial, including a jury trial, is the presumptive mode of dispute resolution, and that the courts must have latitude. The original text acknowledged the need to remain legally compliant, and the altered text removes that, thus creating a legal risk.

perturbation.justification: The omission of "TO THE FULLEST EXTENT PERMITTED BY APPLICABLE LAW" creates a contradiction because it suggests an absolute waiver of jury trial rights, disregarding any legal limitations. The Law Explanation indicates that NY CPLR § 2201 implies that there are scenarios where a jury trial is the presumptive mode of dispute resolution. The original contract acknowledged the need to remain legally compliant, and the altered text removes that safeguard, creating a legal risk. The scraped snippets, both referencing NY CPLR § 2201, state that the court may grant a stay of proceedings. By removing the phrase, the contract now implies an unrestricted waiver that may not be legally enforceable in all circumstances, thus creating a contradiction because the original text, by referencing the law, shows the intent to not usurp the jurisdiction of the courts, and the removal of that phrase demonstrates the opposite, creating a contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: To the fullest extent permitted by law, each Sponsor shall indemnify, defend and hold harmless each other Sponsor and any of its affiliates or any direct or indirect partner, member, shareholder, employee, director, officer or agent of such Sponsor or any of its affiliates from and against any and all losses, liabilities, damages, claims, judgments, awards, settlements, demands, offsets, and expenses (including interest, penalties, court costs, arbitration costs and fees, witness fees and reasonable fees and expenses of outside attorneys, investigators, expert witnesses, accountants and other professionals) arising out of or relating to any material breach or inaccuracy of the representations, warranties or covenants of such Sponsor contained herein.
perturbation.changed_text: Each Sponsor shall indemnify, defend and hold harmless each other Sponsor and any of its affiliates or any direct or indirect partner, member, shareholder, employee, director, officer or agent of such Sponsor or any of its affiliates from and against any and all losses, liabilities, damages, claims, judgments, awards, settlements, demands, offsets, and expenses (including interest, penalties, court costs, arbitration costs and fees, witness fees and reasonable fees and expenses of outside attorneys, investigators, expert witnesses, accountants and other professionals) arising out of or relating to any breach or inaccuracy of the representations, warranties or covenants of such Sponsor contained herein.
perturbation.explanation: The removal of 'To the fullest extent permitted by law' and changing 'material breach' to 'any breach' expands the scope of indemnification beyond what is legally permissible in many jurisdictions. Some states have laws that prohibit indemnification for certain types of breaches, such as those resulting from gross negligence or willful misconduct. By removing the limiting phrase, the contract attempts to provide indemnification even in circumstances where it would be legally prohibited, creating a conflict with applicable law.
perturbation.law_explanation: NY GOL § 5-322.1 generally prevents indemnification clauses from protecting a party from their own negligence when performing construction. The original text acknowledges the need to remain legally compliant, and the altered text removes that, and the change from material breach to any breach creates a legal risk because now even negligible breaches of contract are subject to indemnification.

Skip to main content Legislation Search OpenLegislation Statutes Search Term Search icon Search The Laws of New York Consolidated Laws of New York CHAPTER 24-A General Obligations ARTICLE 5 Creation, Definition and Enforcement of Contractual Obligations TITLE 3 Certain Prohibited Contracts and Provisions of Contracts previous SECTION 5-322 Agreements exempting caterers and catering establishments from liability for negligence void and unenforceable up TITLE 3 Certain Prohibited Contracts and Provisions of Contracts next SECTION 5-322.2 Contents of certain construction contracts This entry was published on 2014-09-22 The selection dates indicate all change milestones for the entire volume, not just the location being viewed. See most recent version before or on: 2014-09-22 2015-10-09 2017-10-27 2018-04-20 2018-07-06 2018-07-13 2018-08-31 2019-01-11 2019-01-18 2019-06-28 2019-07-05 2019-07-19 2019-08-16 2019-10-18 2019-11-22 2019-12-13 2020-02-21 2020-04-10 2020-12-18 2021-01-08 2021-04-02 2021-04-09 2021-06-18 2021-09-24 2021-12-31 2022-02-25 2022-03-04 2022-12-30 2023-01-06 2023-10-27 2023-11-26 2024-12-20 2025-01-03 Share Facebook Email SECTION 5-322.1 Agreements exempting owners and contractors from liability for negligence void and unenforceable; certain cases General Obligations (GOB) CHAPTER 24-A, ARTICLE 5, TITLE 3 § 5-322.1. Agreements exempting owners and contractors from liability for negligence void and unenforceable; certain cases. 1. A covenant, promise, agreement or understanding in, or in connection with or collateral to a contract or agreement relative to the construction, alteration, repair or maintenance of a building, structure, appurtenances and appliances including moving, demolition and excavating connected therewith, purporting to indemnify or hold harmless the promisee against liability for damage arising out of bodily injury to persons or damage to property contributed to, caused by or resulting from the negligence of the promisee, his agents or employees, or indemnitee, whether such negligence be in whole or in part, is against public policy and is void and unenforceable; provided that this section shall not affect the validity of any insurance contract, workers' compensation agreement or other agreement issued by an admitted insurer. This subdivision shall not preclude a promisee requiring indemnification for damages arising out of bodily injury to persons or damage to property caused by or resulting from the negligence of a party other than the promisee, whether or not the promisor is partially negligent. 2. A covenant, promise, agreement or understanding in, or in connection with or collateral to a contract or agreement relative to the construction, alteration, repair or maintenance of a building, structure, appurtenances and appliances including moving, demolition and excavating connected therewith, purporting to condition a subcontractor's or materialman's right to file a claim and/or commence an action on a payment bond on exhaustion of another legal remedy is against public policy and is void and unenforceable; provided that this subdivision shall not affect the validity of any insu
perturbation.justification: The modified contract text removes the 'To the fullest extent permitted by law' clause and broadens the indemnification trigger from 'material breach' to 'any breach.' This creates a potential contradiction with NY GOL § 5-322.1, as it attempts to indemnify parties even for negligence, which the law prohibits in construction-related contracts. The original text limited indemnification to the extent legally permissible, acknowledging legal boundaries. The altered text, by removing this limitation and broadening the scope of breaches covered, now conflicts with the provided snippet, which states that agreements exempting parties from liability for negligence are 'void and unenforceable.'

perturbation.original_text: Each Party hereby represents and warrants to, and agrees with, each other Party that: (a) in connection with the Offer and this Agreement, such Party has not made, and shall not make, any untrue statement of a material fact or omit to state a material fact necessary in order to make the statements made, in the light of the circumstances under which they were made, not misleading; (b) all information provided by or on behalf of such Party to any other Party for inclusion or incorporation by reference in any Schedule TO or other filing with the Commission or any other tender offer material ("Provided Information") was, when provided, true, accurate, correct, and complete in all material respects; provided further, that such Sponsor shall cause such Provided Information and such filing and material, as applicable, to be amended and supplemented, in each case, as required by law and otherwise promptly and as necessary and appropriate to make the Provided Information and such filing and material, as applicable, true, accurate, correct, and complete in all material respects;
perturbation.changed_text: Each Party hereby represents and warrants to, and agrees with, each other Party that: (a) in connection with the Offer and this Agreement, such Party has not made, and shall not make, any untrue statement of a material fact or omit to state a material fact necessary in order to make the statements made, in the light of the circumstances under which they were made, not misleading; (b) all information provided by or on behalf of such Party to any other Party for inclusion or incorporation by reference in any Schedule TO or other filing with the Commission or any other tender offer material ("Provided Information") was, when provided, true, accurate, correct, and complete in all material respects;
perturbation.explanation: Removing the clause 'provided further, that such Sponsor shall cause such Provided Information and such filing and material, as applicable, to be amended and supplemented, in each case, as required by law and otherwise promptly and as necessary and appropriate to make the Provided Information and such filing and material, as applicable, true, accurate, correct, and complete in all material respects' eliminates the explicit obligation to update disclosures as required by law. This omission directly contradicts securities laws that mandate continuous disclosure and the correction of misleading information, potentially exposing the parties to legal liability for incomplete or outdated filings.
perturbation.law_explanation: Rule 12b-20 under the Securities Exchange Act of 1934 requires that filings with the SEC contain all information necessary to ensure that statements made are not misleading. By removing the explicit requirement to amend and supplement filings as required by law, the agreement fails to ensure compliance with this rule and creates a legal risk.

CFR prev | next § 240.12b-20 Additional information. In addition to the information expressly required to be included in a statement or report, there shall be added such further material information, if any, as may be necessary to make the required statements, in the light of the circumstances under which they are made not misleading. perturbation.justification: The modified text removes the explicit obligation to amend and supplement filings, which creates a contradiction with securities laws. Scraped Law Snippet 2 states, "In addition to the information expressly required to be included in a statement or report, there shall be added such further material information, if any, as may be necessary to make the required statements, in the light of the circumstances under which they are made not misleading." By removing the requirement to update filings, the agreement does not ensure that the information remains accurate and not misleading as required by law.
